title: Cancer of Unknown Primary (CUP)
oneLiner: Metastatic malignancy where standard workup fails to identify a primary site; ~3–5% of cancers, poor prognosis, but some subtypes are treatable.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal cord compression** – back pain + leg weakness + sensory level → single finding: loss of perianal sensation/saddle anaesthesia or urinary retention (cauda equina)
- **Superior vena cava obstruction** – facial/neck swelling, distended neck veins, stridor → single finding: stridor or cyanosis despite sitting upright
- **Cardiac tamponade** (e.g., from pericardial metastasis) – hypotension, pulsus paradoxus, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds → single finding: electrical alternans on ECG (rare but pathognomonic)
- **Airway obstruction** (mediastinal mass) – stridor, tachypnoea, cannot lie flat → single finding: biphasic stridor or 'difficulty breathing' that worsens when supine
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms (any combination):
  - Painless lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular, axillary, inguinal)
  - Unexplained weight loss >10% over 3 months
  - Night sweats, fevers (B symptoms)
  - Bone pain (axial) or pathological fracture
  - Hepatomegaly, jaundice, ascites
  - New neurological symptoms (back pain, limb weakness, headache)
- Key exam findings:
  - Palpable lymph nodes (Virchow's node left supraclavicular → suspect gastric)
  - Digital rectal exam / testicular exam (men)
  - Breast exam (women)
  - Skin exam for melanoma/other lesions
  - Spinal tenderness, straight leg raise if back pain
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely**, with 1-line differentiators:
  - **Metastatic cancer from known primary** (e.g., lung, breast, colorectal) – check imaging, tumour markers, prior histology
  - **Lymphoma** – bulky lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, LDH elevated, PET-CT avid
  - **Germ cell tumour** – young male, mediastinal mass, raised AFP/beta-hCG
  - **Sarcoma** – rapid growth, MRI key
  - **Melanoma** – skin lesion, S100 positive on IHC
- **Splitter:** Young male with mediastinal mass + no testicular lump → **germ cell vs lymphoma** – check AFP + beta-hCG (elevated → germ cell; normal → lymphoma)
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Performance status (ECOG 0–4) – crucial for chemo tolerance
  - Urgent ECG if hypotensive/tamponade suspected
  - Blood gas if stridor/respiratory distress
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, LDH, CRP
  - Tumour markers (non-diagnostic but supportive):
    - Men: PSA, AFP, beta-hCG, CEA
    - Women: CA125, CA19-9, CEA, AFP, beta-hCG (if young)
    - Both: LDH (bulk disease), beta-hCG (germ cell/choriocarcinoma)
  - Human chorionic gonadotrophin (hCG) in ALL young patients (rule out choriocarcinoma – treatable)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (CAP) with contrast – look for primary lesion, metastases, nodal stations
  - Second-line: PET-CT – more sensitive for occult primary (especially head/neck, or if CT negative)
  - MRI brain if neurological symptoms or suspected melanoma
  - MRI spine if back pain + neurological signs (cord compression)
- **Special tests:**
  - Biopsy (core needle, not FNA) of the most accessible metastasis – send for:
    - Histology + immunohistochemistry (IHC) panel: CK7, CK20, TTF1, GATA3, CDX2, p63, etc.
    - Molecular testing (e.g., BRAF, MSI, PD-L1) if suspected actionable mutation
    - Germ cell markers if midline mass
- **Diagnostic criteria:** CUP is a diagnosis of exclusion – after thorough clinical exam, imaging (CT CAP ± PET-CT), and IHC, no primary found. If IHC suggests a likely primary, treat as that primary cancer (e.g., CK7+/TTF1+ → lung adenocarcinoma).
- **Never-miss test:** **Beta-hCG** in any young adult with mediastinal/retroperitoneal mass + raised LDH (rules out germ cell tumour – highly curable even with metastases)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Spinal cord compression:** Dexamethasone 16mg IV stat (see Drugs section: [check local policy]) + urgent MRI spine + oncology/orthopaedic referral
  - **SVC obstruction:** Sit patient upright, dexamethasone 8–16mg IV [check local policy], and discuss stenting/radiotherapy with oncology
  - **Tamponade:** Urgent echo, discuss pericardiocentesis with cardiology
  - **Airway compromise:** High-flow oxygen, urgent ENT/anaesthetics review for airway
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Complete staging: CT CAP, PET-CT if indicated
  - Arrange biopsy via interventional radiology or surgical team (same day if possible)
  - Refer to oncology MDT (CUP specialist team if available) – they decide if further IHC/molecular testing needed
  - Start supportive care: pain management, nutrition (dietitian), bisphosphonates if bone metastases and hypercalcaemia
  - Consider palliative care referral early (prognosis often poor)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If IHC suggests a primary (e.g., breast, lung, colorectal) – treat as per that primary's guidelines
  - If truly unknown primary – chemotherapy (platinum-based doublet) per local protocol [check local policy] (e.g., carboplatin + paclitaxel)
  - Newer options: targeted therapy if driver mutation identified (e.g., BRAF, NTRK) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab if TMB-high)
  - Radiotherapy for symptomatic metastases (bone pain, brain)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/hr if nil by mouth for procedure
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if no contraindications, e.g., brain metastases with bleed risk)
  - Diet/NBM status: NBM from midnight if biopsy planned
  - Observations: baseline 4-hourly (NEWS2), increase if unstable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Dexamethasone (for cord compression/SVC obstruction):**
  - Dose: 16mg IV stat – then 8mg IV QDS [check local policy] (taper after definitive treatment)
- **Chemotherapy (first-line for true CUP):**
  - Regimens vary – do not prescribe without oncology input
  - Common: carboplatin AUC5 + paclitaxel 175mg/m² Q3W [check local policy]
- **Supportive medications:**
  - Antiemetics: Ondansetron 8mg IV/PO TDS (if chemotherapy)
  - Bisphosphonates: Zoledronic acid 4mg IV over 15min [check local policy] (for bone metastases – check renal function, correct hypocalcaemia first)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone – avoid in uncontrolled infection (only if life-threatening)
  - Chemotherapy – avoid if ECOG >2 or poor organ function
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin dose based on Calvert formula (GFR required); reduce if renal impairment
  - Bisphosphonates – avoid if CrCl <35 mL/min
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving dexamethasone for suspected cord compression before MRI** – can mask diagnosis (e.g., lymphoma) or delay urgent surgery; **still give it if strong clinical suspicion** (risk of paralysis outweighs risk) but document clearly and arrange MRI ASAP.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypercalcaemia (bone metastases) – check corrected calcium; treat with IV fluids + bisphosphonates
  - Spinal cord compression (as above)
  - SVC obstruction
  - Pathological fracture
  - Malignant effusion (pleural, pericardial, ascites)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cancer cachexia – weight loss, fatigue, appetite loss
  - Venous thromboembolism – high incidence; consider extended VTE prophylaxis
  - Chemotherapy toxicities (neutropenia, neuropathy, nephrotoxicity)
sections.Prognosis:
- Median survival 6–12 months (range 3–24+ months)
- Better if: favourable IHC profile (e.g., breast/lung lineage), good ECOG status (0–1), single metastasis
- Worse if: liver metastases, poor performance status, high LDH, unknown primary that remains 'true CUP'
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a full clinical exam including testicular, breast, and rectal** – can pick up primary in 10–15% of cases.
- **Get enough tissue for IHC + molecular testing** – core biopsy > FNA; send multiple samples if possible.
- **Discuss with oncology early** – they may recommend PET-CT or special stains before biopsy to maximise yield.
- **Consider germ cell tumour in every young male** – beta-hCG and AFP before any intervention.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Starting steroids before biopsy in suspected lymphoma** – risk of obscuring histology; only give if there is evidence of cord compression (life-threatening).
- **Omitting tumour markers in a young patient** – choriocarcinoma is highly curable but requires urgent chemo.
- **Assuming "it's just cancer" without investigating for treatable subtypes** – some primaries (breast, prostate, germ cell) have specific therapies that can prolong life significantly.
- **Using FNA instead of core biopsy** – insufficient tissue for IHC, leading to diagnostic delay.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New back pain with leg weakness; difficulty breathing or stridor; new confusion/seizures; worsening jaundice or abdominal distension; any bleeding (haemoptysis, haematemesis, melaena).
- **Follow-up:** Oncology outpatient appointment within 1 week (or 48h if poor prognosis); arrange transport if performance status poor.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) for:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92%, hypotension)
  - New focal neurology or seizure
  - Stridor or respiratory distress (call anaesthetics/ICU)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or pH <7.3 (sepsis/metabolic crisis)
- **Call crash team if:**
  - GCS <8
  - Respiratory arrest
  - Pulselessness (tamponade, VTE)
  - Anaphylaxis to chemotherapy (rare but immediate)

rawMarkdown:
## Cancer of Unknown Primary (CUP)
**One-liner:** Metastatic malignancy where standard workup fails to identify a primary site; ~3–5% of cancers, poor prognosis, but some subtypes are treatable.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal cord compression** – back pain + leg weakness + sensory level → single finding: loss of perianal sensation/saddle anaesthesia or urinary retention (cauda equina)
- **Superior vena cava obstruction** – facial/neck swelling, distended neck veins, stridor → single finding: stridor or cyanosis despite sitting upright
- **Cardiac tamponade** (e.g., from pericardial metastasis) – hypotension, pulsus paradoxus, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds → single finding: electrical alternans on ECG (rare but pathognomonic)
- **Airway obstruction** (mediastinal mass) – stridor, tachypnoea, cannot lie flat → single finding: biphasic stridor or 'difficulty breathing' that worsens when supine

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms (any combination):
  - Painless lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular, axillary, inguinal)
  - Unexplained weight loss >10% over 3 months
  - Night sweats, fevers (B symptoms)
  - Bone pain (axial) or pathological fracture
  - Hepatomegaly, jaundice, ascites
  - New neurological symptoms (back pain, limb weakness, headache)
- Key exam findings:
  - Palpable lymph nodes (Virchow's node left supraclavicular → suspect gastric)
  - Digital rectal exam / testicular exam (men)
  - Breast exam (women)
  - Skin exam for melanoma/other lesions
  - Spinal tenderness, straight leg raise if back pain

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely**, with 1-line differentiators:
  - **Metastatic cancer from known primary** (e.g., lung, breast, colorectal) – check imaging, tumour markers, prior histology
  - **Lymphoma** – bulky lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, LDH elevated, PET-CT avid
  - **Germ cell tumour** – young male, mediastinal mass, raised AFP/beta-hCG
  - **Sarcoma** – rapid growth, MRI key
  - **Melanoma** – skin lesion, S100 positive on IHC
- **Splitter:** Young male with mediastinal mass + no testicular lump → **germ cell vs lymphoma** – check AFP + beta-hCG (elevated → germ cell; normal → lymphoma)

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Performance status (ECOG 0–4) – crucial for chemo tolerance
  - Urgent ECG if hypotensive/tamponade suspected
  - Blood gas if stridor/respiratory distress
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile, LDH, CRP
  - Tumour markers (non-diagnostic but supportive):
    - Men: PSA, AFP, beta-hCG, CEA
    - Women: CA125, CA19-9, CEA, AFP, beta-hCG (if young)
    - Both: LDH (bulk disease), beta-hCG (germ cell/choriocarcinoma)
  - Human chorionic gonadotrophin (hCG) in ALL young patients (rule out choriocarcinoma – treatable)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (CAP) with contrast – look for primary lesion, metastases, nodal stations
  - Second-line: PET-CT – more sensitive for occult primary (especially head/neck, or if CT negative)
  - MRI brain if neurological symptoms or suspected melanoma
  - MRI spine if back pain + neurological signs (cord compression)
- **Special tests:**
  - Biopsy (core needle, not FNA) of the most accessible metastasis – send for:
    - Histology + immunohistochemistry (IHC) panel: CK7, CK20, TTF1, GATA3, CDX2, p63, etc.
    - Molecular testing (e.g., BRAF, MSI, PD-L1) if suspected actionable mutation
    - Germ cell markers if midline mass
- **Diagnostic criteria:** CUP is a diagnosis of exclusion – after thorough clinical exam, imaging (CT CAP ± PET-CT), and IHC, no primary found. If IHC suggests a likely primary, treat as that primary cancer (e.g., CK7+/TTF1+ → lung adenocarcinoma).
- **Never-miss test:** **Beta-hCG** in any young adult with mediastinal/retroperitoneal mass + raised LDH (rules out germ cell tumour – highly curable even with metastases)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Spinal cord compression:** Dexamethasone 16mg IV stat (see Drugs section: [check local policy]) + urgent MRI spine + oncology/orthopaedic referral
  - **SVC obstruction:** Sit patient upright, dexamethasone 8–16mg IV [check local policy], and discuss stenting/radiotherapy with oncology
  - **Tamponade:** Urgent echo, discuss pericardiocentesis with cardiology
  - **Airway compromise:** High-flow oxygen, urgent ENT/anaesthetics review for airway
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Complete staging: CT CAP, PET-CT if indicated
  - Arrange biopsy via interventional radiology or surgical team (same day if possible)
  - Refer to oncology MDT (CUP specialist team if available) – they decide if further IHC/molecular testing needed
  - Start supportive care: pain management, nutrition (dietitian), bisphosphonates if bone metastases and hypercalcaemia
  - Consider palliative care referral early (prognosis often poor)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If IHC suggests a primary (e.g., breast, lung, colorectal) – treat as per that primary's guidelines
  - If truly unknown primary – chemotherapy (platinum-based doublet) per local protocol [check local policy] (e.g., carboplatin + paclitaxel)
  - Newer options: targeted therapy if driver mutation identified (e.g., BRAF, NTRK) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab if TMB-high)
  - Radiotherapy for symptomatic metastases (bone pain, brain)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 mL/kg/hr if nil by mouth for procedure
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if no contraindications, e.g., brain metastases with bleed risk)
  - Diet/NBM status: NBM from midnight if biopsy planned
  - Observations: baseline 4-hourly (NEWS2), increase if unstable

### Drugs & Doses
- **Dexamethasone (for cord compression/SVC obstruction):**
  - Dose: 16mg IV stat – then 8mg IV QDS [check local policy] (taper after definitive treatment)
- **Chemotherapy (first-line for true CUP):**
  - Regimens vary – do not prescribe without oncology input
  - Common: carboplatin AUC5 + paclitaxel 175mg/m² Q3W [check local policy]
- **Supportive medications:**
  - Antiemetics: Ondansetron 8mg IV/PO TDS (if chemotherapy)
  - Bisphosphonates: Zoledronic acid 4mg IV over 15min [check local policy] (for bone metastases – check renal function, correct hypocalcaemia first)
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone – avoid in uncontrolled infection (only if life-threatening)
  - Chemotherapy – avoid if ECOG >2 or poor organ function
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Carboplatin dose based on Calvert formula (GFR required); reduce if renal impairment
  - Bisphosphonates – avoid if CrCl <35 mL/min
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving dexamethasone for suspected cord compression before MRI** – can mask diagnosis (e.g., lymphoma) or delay urgent surgery; **still give it if strong clinical suspicion** (risk of paralysis outweighs risk) but document clearly and arrange MRI ASAP.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypercalcaemia (bone metastases) – check corrected calcium; treat with IV fluids + bisphosphonates
  - Spinal cord compression (as above)
  - SVC obstruction
  - Pathological fracture
  - Malignant effusion (pleural, pericardial, ascites)
- **Chronic:**
  - Cancer cachexia – weight loss, fatigue, appetite loss
  - Venous thromboembolism – high incidence; consider extended VTE prophylaxis
  - Chemotherapy toxicities (neutropenia, neuropathy, nephrotoxicity)

### Prognosis
- Median survival 6–12 months (range 3–24+ months)
- Better if: favourable IHC profile (e.g., breast/lung lineage), good ECOG status (0–1), single metastasis
- Worse if: liver metastases, poor performance status, high LDH, unknown primary that remains 'true CUP'

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a full clinical exam including testicular, breast, and rectal** – can pick up primary in 10–15% of cases.
- **Get enough tissue for IHC + molecular testing** – core biopsy > FNA; send multiple samples if possible.
- **Discuss with oncology early** – they may recommend PET-CT or special stains before biopsy to maximise yield.
- **Consider germ cell tumour in every young male** – beta-hCG and AFP before any intervention.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Starting steroids before biopsy in suspected lymphoma** – risk of obscuring histology; only give if there is evidence of cord compression (life-threatening).
- **Omitting tumour markers in a young patient** – choriocarcinoma is highly curable but requires urgent chemo.
- **Assuming "it's just cancer" without investigating for treatable subtypes** – some primaries (breast, prostate, germ cell) have specific therapies that can prolong life significantly.
- **Using FNA instead of core biopsy** – insufficient tissue for IHC, leading to diagnostic delay.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New back pain with leg weakness; difficulty breathing or stridor; new confusion/seizures; worsening jaundice or abdominal distension; any bleeding (haemoptysis, haematemesis, melaena).
- **Follow-up:** Oncology outpatient appointment within 1 week (or 48h if poor prognosis); arrange transport if performance status poor.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) for:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92%, hypotension)
  - New focal neurology or seizure
  - Stridor or respiratory distress (call anaesthetics/ICU)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or pH <7.3 (sepsis/metabolic crisis)
- **Call crash team if:**
  - GCS <8
  - Respiratory arrest
  - Pulselessness (tamponade, VTE)
  - Anaphylaxis to chemotherapy (rare but immediate)
